stories.title: Sinner's Shocking R2 Collapse vs. Cerundolo

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text: Sinner led Cerundolo 6-3, 6-2, 5-1 at Roland Garros — then cramps. No. 56 Argentine claimed 18 of the final 20 games. Final: 3-6, 2-6, 7-5, 6-1, 6-1. World No. 1 is out. #RolandGarros #Tennis

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text: Jim Courier on Sinner's Roland Garros exit: "This is absolute baloney. Rules are being bent." Chris Evert: Sinner "shouldn't have been allowed to leave court." Cerundolo wins last 3 sets 7-5, 6-1, 6-1. #RolandGarros #Tennis

facebook.long_form:
**Window:** 8:00 AM ET

He was two sets up, 5-1 in the third, one game from the round of 16. And then it all fell apart.

World No. 1 Jannik Sinner — the man who swept all five Masters 1000 titles this year, the overwhelming Roland Garros favorite — was eliminated in the second round on Thursday by No. 56 Juan Manuel Cerundolo. Final score: 3-6, 2-6, 7-5, 6-1, 6-1.

The collapse started with cramps in the brutal Paris heat (33°C / 91°F). Courtside microphones captured Sinner saying he felt "dizzy and nauseous." He required a medical timeout at 5-4, 0-40 in the third — and that decision immediately sparked controversy. Jim Courier on TNT was direct: "This is absolute baloney. Rules are being bent." Chris Evert said Sinner "shouldn't have been allowed to leave court."

The timeout didn't help. Cerundolo claimed 18 of the final 20 games in one of the most dramatic collapses this tournament has ever seen. He becomes the lowest-ranked player to beat a world No. 1 at Roland Garros since 1998.

For Sinner, the career Grand Slam quest — Roland Garros was the only major he hadn't won — will wait until 2027.

What did you think of the medical timeout controversy?

---
facebook.image_caption:
**Window:** 8:00 AM ET

From domination to collapse in the Paris heat. World No. 1 Sinner exits Roland Garros R2. Cerundolo wins last three sets 7-5, 6-1, 6-1 after Sinner led 6-3, 6-2, 5-1. Biggest upset of 2026.

article.headline: Sinner's Dream Dies at Roland Garros: World No. 1 Crumbles in Heat, Exits in Five Sets

stories.title: Fonseca Stuns Djokovic in Five-Set Epic

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text: Fonseca was "being destroyed" by Djokovic — down 0-2 sets. Nearly 5 hours later: 4-6, 4-6, 6-3, 7-5, 7-5. The 19-year-old's fifth set was "all heart." Three-time champion Djokovic is out. #RolandGarros #Tennis

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text: Fonseca: first teenager in 30 years to complete back-to-back 2-set comebacks at a Grand Slam. Only the 2nd time Djokovic has lost from 2-0 up — both times at Roland Garros (2010 vs Melzer). #RolandGarros #Tennis

facebook.long_form:
**Window:** 12:30 PM ET

For nearly two hours, it looked like a lesson. Novak Djokovic won the first set, won the second, and the 19-year-old Brazilian admitted afterward that the three-time champion "was destroying me."

Then came four hours and 53 minutes of tennis history.

Joao Fonseca rallied from 0-2 sets down to defeat Djokovic 4-6, 4-6, 6-3, 7-5, 7-5 on Court Philippe-Chatrier on Friday — one of the most extraordinary third-round results this tournament has ever produced. It is only the second time in Djokovic's career that he has lost from two sets up. Both times, it has happened at Roland Garros.

The stats are remarkable: Fonseca is the first teenager in 30 years to complete back-to-back comebacks from two sets down at a Grand Slam. He already did it against Prizmic in round two. The fifth set, he said, "was all heart."

Djokovic embraced him warmly at the net. In his press conference: "The level was amazing."

Have you ever watched a comeback like that?

---
facebook.image_caption:
**Window:** 12:30 PM ET

Joao Fonseca (19) def. Novak Djokovic (3x champion) 4-6, 4-6, 6-3, 7-5, 7-5 in 4h53m. Back-to-back 5-set comebacks from 0-2. Historic. #RolandGarros

article.headline: Fonseca Defeats Djokovic in Roland Garros Epic: 19-Year-Old Completes Historic Comeback

stories.title: Open Era First — Men's R16 Has No Former Champions

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text: For the first time in the Open Era, Roland Garros men's R16 has no former Grand Slam champions. Sinner out R2. Djokovic out R3. Just 2 of the top 8 seeds remain. Zverev is the new favorite. #RolandGarros #Tennis

facebook.long_form:
**Window:** 6:00 PM ET

In more than 50 years of Open Era tennis at Roland Garros, the men's round of 16 has always included at least one former Grand Slam champion. This year, for the first time ever, it will not.

Sinner — the world No. 1 and the tournament favorite — fell to No. 56 Cerundolo in round two on Thursday. Djokovic — the three-time champion — fell to 19-year-old Fonseca in round three on Friday. Defending champion Alcaraz withdrew before the tournament with a wrist injury.

Just two of the original top eight seeds remain. The clear frontrunner now is No. 2 seed Alexander Zverev, who advanced to the round of 16 with a 6-4, 6-3, 5-7, 6-2 victory over Quentin Halys on Saturday.

But in a draw this unusual, nothing is guaranteed. Fonseca — who came back from 0-2 twice in his first three matches — is capturing the tournament's imagination. The men's title at Roland Garros 2026 is genuinely up for grabs.

Who do you think wins the men's title now?

---
facebook.image_caption:
**Window:** 6:00 PM ET

Open Era first: Roland Garros men's R16 has no former Grand Slam champions. Sinner out R2. Djokovic out R3. Just 2 of the top 8 seeds remain. Zverev is the new favorite.

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text: Swiatek 6-4, 6-4 over Linette in 85 min at Roland Garros — the first all-Polish match in Open Era history here. Three-time champion "looking like her old dominant self." Next: Kostyuk in R4. #RolandGarros #Tennis

facebook.long_form:
**Window:** 3:00 PM ET

In more than 50 years of Open Era tennis at Roland Garros, two Polish players had never faced each other here. On Friday, that changed — and the result was exactly what the world expected.

Iga Swiatek, the three-time champion and third seed, dismantled compatriot Magda Linette 6-4, 6-4 in just 85 minutes on Court Philippe-Chatrier. Observers described Swiatek as "looking like her old dominant self" — a meaningful phrase for a player who has faced extra scrutiny heading into this tournament.

Linette, ranked No. 47, made the early games competitive. But Swiatek's superiority was increasingly apparent as the match went on. She broke twice in each set and was rarely troubled.

Swiatek now faces Ukrainian Marta Kostyuk in the fourth round. The women's draw — with Sabalenka, Gauff, and Rybakina all still competing — is building toward a genuinely exciting second week.

Are you expecting another Swiatek Roland Garros title this year?

---
facebook.image_caption:
**Window:** 3:00 PM ET

First all-Polish match in Open Era history at Roland Garros: Swiatek 6-4, 6-4 over Linette in 85 minutes. Three-time champion looking dominant. Next up: Kostyuk in R4.

article.headline: Swiatek Dominant in Historic All-Polish Battle, Advances to Roland Garros Fourth Round

stories.title: Day 7 Women's Action — Sabalenka, Andreeva Advance; Gauff and Osaka in R3

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text: Day 7 Roland Garros: Sabalenka through. Andreeva through. Gauff (9-match Paris win streak) vs Potapova and Osaka vs Jovic both in R3 on Lenglen. The women's second week is almost set. #RolandGarros #Tennis

facebook.long_form:
**Window:** 9:00 PM ET

Saturday at Roland Garros brought advancing victories for the world's top seed — and left two fascinating third-round matches still to be decided.

No. 1 seed Aryna Sabalenka moved into the round of 16 with a victory over Daria Kasatkina. Sabalenka has never won Roland Garros but reached the 2025 final, and she is building toward what could be a defining run in the second week.

Mirra Andreeva, the 18-year-old Russian who has been one of the tour's best clay performers this season, also advanced past Marie Bouzkova.

Meanwhile, defending champion Coco Gauff — riding a nine-match Paris winning streak — faced Anastasia Potapova in the third round. And Naomi Osaka, in her first Roland Garros third round since 2019, played 19-year-old American Iva Jovic on Lenglen.

The women's second week is almost fully set. Sabalenka, Swiatek, and Gauff are all positioned to make deep runs — and a blockbuster final is shaping up.

Which women's player are you most excited to watch in the second week?

---
facebook.image_caption:
**Window:** 9:00 PM ET

Day 7: Sabalenka through. Andreeva through. Gauff and Osaka both in R3 action on Lenglen. The women's draw at Roland Garros is taking shape for a brilliant second week.
